B0_600.rules.how_pieces_move.intro: Every piece moves in its own way. The rook moves in straight lines along ranks and files; the bishop moves diagonally; the queen combines both and is the strongest piece. The knight jumps in an L-shape (two squares one way, then one square sideways) and is the only piece that can hop over others. The king moves one square in any direction, and pawns move straight forward but capture diagonally. A piece captures by moving onto a square occupied by an enemy piece and removing it.

examples.title: The starting position
examples.explanation: Both armies are lined up. Back rank from a-file: rook, knight, bishop, queen, king, bishop, knight, rook. Note the queen starts on her own colour (white queen on a light square).

examples.title: Knight's reach
examples.explanation: The white knight on d5 can jump to any of eight L-shaped squares: b6, c7, e7, f6, f4, e3, c3, and b4. Notice it never lands on a square next to itself.

examples.title: Making a capture
examples.explanation: The white rook on d1 slides up the d-file and captures the black bishop on d3 by landing on its square. The rook moves in a straight line, so nothing may stand between d1 and d3.

keyPoints: Rook = straight lines, bishop = diagonals, queen = both.
The knight moves in an L-shape and is the only piece that jumps over others.
Pawns advance straight ahead but capture one square diagonally forward.
You capture by landing on an enemy piece's square and removing it.
Pieces (except the knight) cannot move through other pieces.

B0_600.rules.special_moves.intro: Three special rules go beyond ordinary moves. Castling moves the king two squares toward a rook and jumps that rook to the king's other side, tucking the king to safety; it is only legal if neither piece has moved, the squares between are empty, and the king is not moving into, through, or out of check. En passant lets a pawn capture an enemy pawn that just advanced two squares and landed beside it, as if it had moved only one. Promotion happens when a pawn reaches the far end of the board and is immediately replaced by a queen, rook, bishop, or knight of its own colour, almost always a queen.

examples.title: Ready to castle
examples.explanation: Both sides can castle on either wing: the squares between king and rook are empty and nothing is in check. White kingside (O-O) puts the king on g1 and rook on f1.

examples.title: En passant capture
examples.explanation: Black's pawn just went d7-d5, landing beside White's e5 pawn. White may play exd6, capturing as if the black pawn had only moved to d6. This is allowed only right now.

examples.title: One step from a new queen
examples.explanation: The white pawn on a7 plays a8 and promotes. Choosing a queen gives White an overwhelming new piece on the board.

keyPoints: Castling needs an unmoved king and rook, empty squares between them, and a king that is not in or passing through check.
En passant is only available on the very next move after the enemy pawn's two-square advance.
A pawn that reaches the last rank must promote, usually to a queen.
Castling is the only move where two of your pieces move at once.
Promotion can create a second queen, instantly transforming the game.

B0_600.rules.check_mate_stalemate.intro: Check means the king is under attack and must be dealt with immediately. You can escape check in exactly three ways: move the king to a safe square, block the attack by putting a piece in the way, or capture the attacking piece. Checkmate is check with no legal escape at all, and it ends the game. Stalemate is different: the side to move is NOT in check but has no legal move, and the game is an immediate draw. When you are winning by a lot, watch out, accidentally leaving the enemy king with no moves while it is not in check throws away the win.

examples.title: Not in check, plenty to do
examples.explanation: A calm position to contrast with the others: White's king on e1 is safe and has many legal moves. Use it to picture what 'no check, free to move' looks like before studying mate and stalemate.

examples.title: Checkmate
examples.explanation: The black king on h8 is checked by the white knight on f7. It cannot move (g8 has the rook, h7/g7 have pawns), the check cannot be blocked, and the knight is too far to capture. That is mate.

examples.title: Stalemate, a wasted win
examples.explanation: Black is NOT in check, but the king on h8 has no legal move: g8, h7, and g7 are all covered by the queen on g6. With no other pieces, Black has no move at all, so the game is a draw despite White's huge material lead.

keyPoints: Escape check by moving the king, blocking, or capturing the attacker.
Checkmate = the king is in check and has no legal escape; the game is over.
Stalemate = no legal move while NOT in check; it is a draw, not a win.
Always check whether the opponent has any legal move before you think you've won.
When far ahead, leave the losing king at least one safe square until you can mate it.

B0_600.rules.values_and_trades.intro: To judge trades, give each piece a point value: pawn 1, knight 3, bishop 3, rook 5, queen 9. The king has no number because it can never be lost. A trade is good when you come out ahead in points (for example, winning a rook for a bishop) and bad when you come out behind. Before any capture, ask a simple question: if I take, what can recapture, and do I end up with more material than I gave? Never hand over a piece for nothing.

examples.title: Equal material, even trade
examples.explanation: Both sides have developed a knight and pushed the e-pawn. If a knight takes a knight, the other side recaptures and material stays equal, a fair trade.

examples.title: A clean, equal swap
examples.explanation: Bishop and knight are each worth 3 points. The white bishop on d3 can capture the black knight on d5, and if Black could recapture it would be an even 3-for-3 trade. Trading like-for-like keeps material balanced.

keyPoints: Standard values: pawn 1, knight 3, bishop 3, rook 5, queen 9.
A trade is good if you gain points and bad if you lose them.
Winning a rook for a minor piece (5 for 3) is 'winning the exchange'.
Before capturing, count every attacker and defender of the square.
Two minor pieces (about 6) are usually worth more than a single rook (5).

B0_600.mate.mate_in_one.intro: A mate in one is a single move that checkmates the enemy king right now. To find it, look for forcing checks and then test whether the king has any escape. Two questions confirm it: does the king have any safe square to run to, and can the check be blocked or the checking piece captured? If the answer to all of those is no, you have found mate. Always make sure the piece delivering the check is itself safe (or supported) so it cannot simply be taken.

examples.title: Back-rank mate
examples.explanation: White plays Ra8. The black king on g8 is trapped by its own f7, g7, h7 pawns and cannot escape the check from the rook on the open back rank. That is mate in one.

examples.title: Queen mate with king support
examples.explanation: White plays Qe1-e... the direct idea: with the white king on e6 controlling d7, e7, and f7, the queen can deliver mate on the e-file or 8th rank. For example Qh8 checks the e8 king, which has no flight squares because the white king guards the escape route.

examples.title: Two-rook ladder mate
examples.explanation: White plays Rb3 (or Ra3-style ladder): one rook checks along a rank while the other cuts off the rank above, leaving the black king on e3 no escape. This is the basic ladder finish with two rooks.

keyPoints: A mate in one is a check the king cannot escape, block, or capture away.
Scan all your possible checks first, then test each for escapes.
Confirm the king has zero safe squares before declaring mate.
Make sure the checking piece is protected or out of reach.
Back-rank and edge-of-board kings are the most common mating targets.

B0_600.mate.overkill_mates.intro: With a big material edge like king and queen, or king and two rooks, against a lone king, the plan is the same: drive the enemy king to the edge of the board and mate it there. With two rooks you use the 'ladder', one rook cuts off a rank while the other checks, then they leapfrog the king toward the edge, and you don't even need your own king. With king and queen, the queen herds the king to the edge while your own king comes up to support the final blow. The big danger is stalemate: keep the queen at least a knight's distance away until your king arrives, so the lone king always has a square until it is truly mated.

examples.title: King and queen: kings face off
examples.explanation: With the white king on e6 directly opposing the black king on e8, the queen delivers mate on the 8th rank (Qe1-e8 is blocked by support; the standard finish is Qe7 covered by the king or a back-rank queen check). The kings standing in opposition is the key to boxing in the lone king.

examples.title: Two rooks: building the ladder
examples.explanation: The rook on a7 fences the black king onto the 8th rank, and the rook on b5 is ready to check on b8. White plays Rb8 to deliver mate, the classic two-rook ladder finish on the edge.

examples.title: King-and-queen mate delivered
examples.explanation: The white queen on d7 gives check and the white king on d6 guards the queen and the squares c7 and e7. The black king on d8 has no escape, this is the finished king-and-queen mate. Study how the supporting king makes it work.

keyPoints: Goal: push the lone king to the edge or corner, then mate.
Two rooks ladder the king to the edge without needing your own king.
King and queen: use the queen to confine, your king to deliver mate.
Beware stalemate, leave the king a square until you can actually mate.
Keep the queen a knight's-move away from the lone king to avoid stalemate.

B0_600.mate.k_r_vs_k.intro: King and rook beat a lone king with a simple, repeatable technique. The rook draws a 'box' that the enemy king cannot cross, shrinking the king's space step by step. Your own king walks up to stand directly in front of the enemy king, taking the 'opposition' (the two kings face off with one square between them). With the kings in opposition, the rook delivers check on the back rank for mate. If the kings are not yet opposed, make a quiet 'waiting' rook move to force the enemy king to step aside, then take the opposition.

examples.title: Kings in opposition, rook ready
examples.explanation: The white king on d6 opposes the black king on d8. The rook on h2 now plays Rh8 to deliver checkmate on the back rank, the black king has no escape because its own edge and the white king cover every square.

examples.title: The finished rook mate
examples.explanation: With the white king on e6 opposing the black king on e8, the rook on a8 checks along the back rank. The king cannot go to d7, e7, f7 (white king), nor d8 or f8 (rook), so it is mate. This is the goal position of the whole technique.

examples.title: Opposition first, then check
examples.explanation: Kings oppose on the e-file. White plays Ra8 to deliver the back-rank mate. If the kings were not yet opposed, White would first make a waiting rook move to force Black into opposition.

keyPoints: The rook makes a wall (box) the enemy king cannot step past.
Walk your king up to take the opposition, face to face, one square apart.
Mate comes when the rook checks the king pinned against the edge.
Use a waiting rook move to pass the move and gain the opposition.
Keep the rook safe from the enemy king while you maneuver.

B0_600.safety.free_capture.intro: A free capture wins an enemy piece that has no defender, you take it and nothing can recapture. Before grabbing it, check two things: is the target really undefended, and is the piece you use to capture safe afterward? Just as important, do the same check for your own pieces every move: make sure none of yours is hanging (attacked and undefended) before your opponent notices. Spotting free material for both sides is the single biggest skill at the beginner level.

examples.title: Grab the hanging queen
examples.explanation: The black queen on d5 sits on the long diagonal with no defender. White plays Bxd5 (g2-f3-e4-d5), winning the queen for free, the bishop is in no danger afterward.

examples.title: Win the undefended rook
examples.explanation: The black rook on a6 is undefended and sits on the white bishop's diagonal. White plays Bxa6, taking a 5-point rook for nothing. Always scan the long diagonals for loose pieces.

keyPoints: A piece is 'hanging' when it is attacked and has no defender.
Before capturing, confirm nothing can recapture your piece.
Check your own pieces for hangers every single move.
Free material usually decides games at the beginner level.
Count attackers and defenders of a square before you trade or take.

B0_600.safety.fork_intro.intro: A fork is one move that attacks two (or more) enemy pieces at once. Because the opponent can only save one, you win the other. The knight is the classic forking piece because of its tricky L-shaped jumps, but queens, bishops, rooks, and even pawns can fork too. The most powerful version is the 'royal fork', attacking the king and queen together: the king must escape the check, and then you capture the queen. When you have a fork available, choose the one that wins the most valuable piece.

examples.title: Royal fork with the knight
examples.explanation: White plays Nc7+, forking the black king on a8 and the queen on e8. The king must respond to the check, and then White plays Nxe8 to win the queen.

examples.title: Knight forks two rooks
examples.explanation: The white knight on e6 attacks both black rooks (d8 and f8) at the same time. Black can save only one, so White wins a rook next move.

examples.title: Pawn fork
examples.explanation: The white pawn on d4 attacks both the rook on c5 and the knight on e5 (pawns capture diagonally). One push has forked two pieces, White wins material.

keyPoints: A fork attacks two targets at once; the opponent can only save one.
Knights are the most common forkers thanks to their L-shaped jumps.
A royal fork hits the king and queen, the king must move, then you take the queen.
Look for two undefended or high-value enemy pieces a knight's move apart.
Pick the fork that wins the most material.

B0_600.safety.defend_hanging.intro: When one of your pieces is attacked, you have four ways to save it: add a defender so a capture would just be an even trade, move the piece to a safe square, block the line of attack, or capture the attacker. Before reacting, count attackers against defenders, if a piece is attacked once and defended once, it may already be safe. Choose the response that loses the least and ideally improves your position, for example moving the piece somewhere active rather than passively backing it up.

examples.title: Add a defender
examples.explanation: The black rook on d8 attacks the undefended white bishop on d3. White plays Rd1, defending the bishop down the d-file, now a capture would simply be met by a recapture, so the bishop is safe.

examples.title: Move it to safety
examples.explanation: The black rook on d5 attacks the white bishop on d3. With no defender available, White simply moves the bishop off the d-file (for example Bf5 or Be2), saving it cleanly.

examples.title: Capture the attacker
examples.explanation: The black rook on d4 attacks down the open d-file. White's rook on d1 can play Rxd4, capturing the attacking rook outright, the most direct way to end the threat.

keyPoints: Four ways to save a piece: defend it, move it, block, or capture the attacker.
Count attackers vs defenders, an equally defended piece may be safe.
Moving the piece to an active square can defend and improve at once.
Blocking only works against line pieces (rook, bishop, queen).
Capturing the attacker is often the cleanest defense if it's safe.

B600_1000.tactics.pin.intro: A pin happens when a piece is attacked along a line (a rank, file, or diagonal) and cannot move without exposing a more valuable piece behind it. When the piece behind is the king, the pinned piece is completely frozen and is not allowed to move at all (an absolute pin). When the piece behind is just more valuable, like a queen, moving is legal but loses material (a relative pin). Because a pinned piece is stuck, it becomes a great target: pile more attackers onto it and win it.

examples.title: The Ruy Lopez pin idea
examples.explanation: The white bishop on b5 pins the knight on c6 against the rook on a8 (and pressures it). Black often plays a6 to question the bishop. Notice how the c6 knight is awkward while the bishop eyes it down the diagonal.

examples.title: Absolute pin on the e-file
examples.explanation: The black knight on e5 is pinned to its king on e8 by the rook on e1. The knight cannot legally move. This is the moment to attack it with another piece and win it.

examples.title: Pile up on the pinned piece
examples.explanation: The black bishop on e5 is pinned to the e8 king by the rook on e1, and the queen on e3 adds a second attacker. The bishop is frozen and outnumbered, so White wins it (for example Qxe5+ or Rxe5+ when the moment is right).

keyPoints: Only line pieces pin: bishops, rooks, and queens.
A piece pinned against the king cannot move at all (absolute pin).
Attack a pinned piece with extra attackers it cannot run from.
Watch your own side too: do not leave defenders or key pieces pinned.

B600_1000.tactics.skewer.intro: A skewer is the reverse of a pin: a valuable piece is attacked in front, and when it moves out of the way, a lesser piece behind it on the same line is captured. Skewers are most powerful with check, because the king is the most valuable piece and is forced to step aside, abandoning whatever stands behind it. Look for an enemy king and a rook or queen lined up on the same rank, file, or diagonal.

examples.title: Skewer along the back rank
examples.explanation: Play Rh8+. The black king on e8 must leave the back rank, and then Rxa8 wins the rook. King in front, rook behind, on the same rank.

examples.title: Diagonal skewer with the bishop
examples.explanation: Play Bf1+. The check hits the king on b5 along the f1-a6 diagonal; the king steps off, and Bxa6 wins the queen sitting behind it.

keyPoints: Skewer: the more valuable piece is in front and must move, exposing the one behind.
A check is the strongest skewer because the king must move.
Pin vs skewer: in a pin the valuable piece is behind; in a skewer it is in front.
Hunt for a king and a rook or queen on the same line.

B600_1000.tactics.forks.intro: A fork is a single move that attacks two or more enemy targets at once, so the opponent can only save one. The knight is the classic forking piece because it jumps and attacks in an L-shape that other pieces cannot block or easily defend. A royal fork hits the king and queen at the same time: the king must move and the queen falls. Pawns fork too, by pushing forward to attack two pieces diagonally.

examples.title: Knight royal fork
examples.explanation: The knight on e6 attacks both the king on e8 and the queen on d8. Because it is check, Black must move the king and White plays Nxd8, winning the queen.

examples.title: Knight forks two rooks
examples.explanation: The knight on e6 attacks both rooks, on d8 and f8, at the same time. Black can defend or move only one, so White grabs the other next move.

examples.title: Pawn fork
examples.explanation: Play d4-d5. The pawn now attacks both the rook on c5 and the knight on e5. The opponent can save only one piece, so White wins material with a humble pawn push.

keyPoints: A fork attacks two targets; only one can be saved.
Knights are the best forkers because their attack cannot be blocked.
The royal fork (king and queen) wins the queen by force.
A pawn push can fork two pieces on the diagonals in front of it.

B600_1000.tactics.discovered_attack.intro: A discovered attack happens when you move one piece out of the way to unleash an attack from a long-range piece (rook, bishop, or queen) standing behind it. The power is doubled: the piece that moves can make its own threat while the uncovered piece attacks something else. The strongest version is the discovered check, where moving the front piece gives check from behind, so the opponent must respond to the check while your moving piece grabs material.

examples.title: Discovered check
examples.explanation: The bishop on e4 stands in front of the rook on e1. Move the bishop anywhere (for example Bh7) and the rook delivers check to the king on e8 along the open e-file. While the king deals with the check, the bishop has gone where it likes.

examples.title: Discovery wins the queen
examples.explanation: Play Nxb5. The knight captures the queen and at the same time uncovers the bishop on a1, which now checks the king on h8. Because it is check, Black cannot recapture the knight, so White stays a queen up.

keyPoints: Two threats at once: the moving piece and the unmasked piece both attack.
Discovered check forces the opponent to deal with the check first.
The front (moving) piece can capture or attack freely, even something defended.
Set them up by lining a rook, bishop, or queen behind your own piece.

B600_1000.mating.back_rank.intro: A back-rank mate happens when a king is trapped on its own first rank by its own pawns, and a rook or queen checks along that rank with no escape. It is one of the most common mates in real games, because castled kings often sit behind three unmoved pawns. The defense is to make luft (a little air) by pushing one of those pawns so the king has an escape square. On offense, you can sometimes remove the piece that defends the back rank and then deliver mate.

examples.title: Classic back-rank mate
examples.explanation: Play Ra8#. The rook checks along the eighth rank, and the king on g8 is hemmed in by its own pawns on f7, g7, and h7 with no escape. This is mate.

examples.title: Luft saves the king
examples.explanation: Here Black has played h6, creating luft. Now Ra8+ is only a check: the king escapes to h7. This shows exactly why giving your king an escape square prevents back-rank disasters.

keyPoints: The king is mated on its back rank, boxed in by its own pawns.
A rook or queen on the back rank with no defender delivers the mate.
Make luft (push a pawn) so your king has an escape square.
Removing the defender of the back rank can turn a check into mate.

B600_1000.mating.mate_in_two.intro: A mate in two means you force checkmate within two of your own moves, no matter how the opponent replies. The key skill is calculation: find your first move, then check that every legal black answer still allows a mate next move. The best first moves are usually the most forcing ones, but sometimes the winning idea is a quiet move that sets up an unstoppable threat. Always confirm that every defensive try is covered before you commit.

examples.title: Two-rook ladder mate
examples.explanation: Play Rb8+ (1.Rb2-b8+). The king must run to the seventh rank (Kh7 or Kf7), and then the other rook delivers mate on the seventh, for example Ra7#. The rooks climb like a ladder, one always checking while the other guards.

keyPoints: Mate in two = forced mate by your second move against any defense.
Start with the most forcing candidate (check, capture, or strong threat).
Check every legal reply: the mate must work against all of them.
A quiet first move can be the key if it leaves no defense.

B600_1000.mating.smothered_mate.intro: A smothered mate is delivered by a knight when the enemy king is completely surrounded by its own pieces and has no escape square. The classic way to force it is a queen sacrifice: you check the king, drive it into the corner, then sacrifice the queen to force one of the king's own pieces to block the last escape square. With the king now smothered, the knight gives check and it is mate. The whole sequence is known as Philidor's Legacy.

examples.title: The queen sacrifice that forces it
examples.explanation: Play Qg8+!! The rook is forced to capture with Rxg8 (it is the only legal move). Now the king on h8 is smothered by its own rook on g8 and pawns on g7 and h7, and Nf7# is checkmate.

examples.title: The finished smothered mate
examples.explanation: This is the mating picture (Black to move and already mated). The knight on f7 checks the king on h8, which is boxed in by its own rook on g8 and pawns on g7 and h7. There is no escape and no capture: smothered mate.

keyPoints: Only a knight can mate a king smothered by its own pieces.
The king must have no flight squares: all are blocked by friendly pieces.
A queen sacrifice often forces a defender to plug the last escape square.
Recognize the pattern: king in the corner, knight ready to jump in.

B600_1000.openings.principles.intro: Good opening play comes down to three priorities: control the center, develop your pieces, and get your king safe by castling. Fight for the central squares with a pawn (such as e4 or d4) because central control gives your pieces more range. Develop knights and bishops quickly toward the center, and try not to move the same piece twice or bring the queen out too early. Castle within the first ten moves to tuck your king away and connect your rooks.

examples.title: The starting position
examples.explanation: From here, a principled first move grabs the center: 1.e4 or 1.d4 opens lines for a bishop and the queen and claims central space. The plan is center, then development, then castling.

examples.title: Principles fulfilled (for White)
examples.explanation: White has a center pawn on e4, both minor pieces developed (knight f3, bishop c4), and has castled. This is a healthy opening picture. Black is a tempo behind and should now finish developing and castle too.

keyPoints: Stake a claim in the center with a pawn early.
Develop knights and bishops fast; usually knights before bishops.
Castle early to make your king safe and activate a rook.
Avoid moving the same piece twice or bringing the queen out too soon.

B600_1000.openings.italian_game.intro: The Italian Game begins 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4, a classical opening that follows the opening principles to the letter. White takes the center, develops a knight, and aims the bishop at f7, the weakest point in Black's camp. From here White usually castles and prepares the central break d4 to open the position. It is an excellent first opening because the ideas are natural and the resulting positions are easy to understand.

examples.title: The Italian setup (Giuoco Piano)
examples.explanation: After 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4 Bc5, both sides mirror each other. White's bishop eyes f7 and the knight controls the center. White will castle and look to play c3 and d4.

examples.title: Preparing the d4 break
examples.explanation: White has played c3, building a pawn duo and preparing d2-d4 to strike at the center. This d4 break is the key plan of the Italian, opening lines for the better-developed white pieces.

keyPoints: Main line: 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4.
The bishop on c4 targets the sensitive f7 square.
Castle quickly and aim for the central break d4.
Natural development makes it ideal for learning sound opening play.

B600_1000.openings.london_system.intro: The London System is a solid setup for White after 1.d4 where you develop the same way against almost anything Black does. The signature move is bringing the dark-squared bishop out to f4 before playing e3, so it is active outside the pawn chain rather than trapped behind it. White then completes a sturdy structure with pawns on d4 and e3, knights on f3 and d2, the bishop on d3, and short castling. It is easy to learn because the moves repeat in many games.

examples.title: The London bishop is out
examples.explanation: White has played d4, Bf4, and e3. The key point: the dark-squared bishop is developed to f4 outside the pawn chain before e3 locked it in. This is the heart of the London setup.

examples.title: A complete London structure
examples.explanation: Both sides have developed their light bishops outside the chain. White's pieces sit on their natural London squares and will follow with Bd3 and castling. The plan is a steady kingside buildup or the central c4 break.

keyPoints: A 1.d4 system you can play against most black replies.
Develop the dark-squared bishop to f4 before playing e3.
Standard setup: pawns d4 and e3, bishop d3, knights f3 and d2, then castle.
Solid and low-theory, but you must still know the typical plans.

B600_1000.endgame.opposition.intro: The opposition is a king battle in pawn endgames: when the two kings face each other with one empty square between them, the player who does NOT have to move holds the opposition and controls the key squares. Whoever must move has to give ground. Taking the opposition lets your king force the enemy king backward, which is how you escort a pawn to promotion or hold a defense. When a direct standoff will not break through, you can outflank by stepping sideways to win the squares you need.

examples.title: Direct opposition
examples.explanation: The kings face each other on e6 and e4 with e5 between them. It is Black to move, so White holds the opposition: Black must step aside and White's king will gain ground. Whoever is forced to move must give way.

examples.title: Opposition to escort a pawn
examples.explanation: White wants to advance the e-pawn, but the king must lead. Winning the opposition (so Black has to move and yield squares) lets the white king clear the path in front of the pawn toward promotion.

keyPoints: Opposition: kings face off with one square between them; having the move is a disadvantage.
The side not to move holds the opposition and gains ground.
Use the opposition to push the enemy king back and clear a path.
If a direct push stalls, outflank by going around the side.

B600_1000.endgame.promoting_a_pawn.intro: Turning a pawn into a queen is often how endgames are won. With a king and pawn against a lone king, the king must lead the way, controlling the squares in front of the pawn so it cannot be stopped. In a race where both sides push passed pawns, count tempos carefully: use the square of the pawn to see at a glance whether the enemy king can catch it. After you queen, finish cleanly and watch out for stalemate, which throws away the win when the enemy king has no moves but is not in check.

examples.title: King must lead the pawn
examples.explanation: Pushing the pawn immediately lets the black king blockade it. Instead the white king must go first to fight for the squares in front of the pawn (using the opposition) so the pawn can advance safely to promotion.

examples.title: The pawn race
examples.explanation: Both sides have a far-advanced passer. White is to move and queens first with a8=Q, arriving a full tempo ahead of Black's pawn. Counting who queens first, and with check if possible, decides the race.

examples.title: Queen without stalemate
examples.explanation: White promotes with e8=Q. Note that the king on g7 still has free squares (such as h6 or h7), so this is not stalemate. Always check that the enemy king keeps a legal move when you queen so you do not accidentally stalemate.

keyPoints: The king must escort the pawn, taking the squares in front of it.
In a pawn race, count moves and use the 'square of the pawn' to judge it.
A passed pawn supported by its king is very hard to stop.
After queening, avoid stalemate: always leave the enemy king a move or give check.

B1000_1400.combos.deflection.intro: Deflection means forcing an enemy piece away from an important job. A piece may be guarding a square, a mating threat, or another piece. If you can attack that piece and make it move (often with a check or a capture it must answer), the thing it was protecting is suddenly undefended. Then you strike there.

examples.title: Overloaded back-rank defender
examples.explanation: Black's rook on d8 is the only thing stopping Re8 mate. White plays Rxd8+; the rook is deflected (it must recapture or the king is mated), and White follows with Re8#. The d8 rook could not both guard the back rank and stay put.

examples.title: King boxed in by its own pawns
examples.explanation: The black king on g8 is shut in by the f7, g7 and h7 pawns. With nothing defending the back rank, Re8+ or Ra8+ is decisive. This is the picture a deflection tries to create: pull the lone defender off the back rank, then mate along it.

keyPoints: First ask: what is each enemy piece defending? The busiest piece is your target.
A forcing move (check or a capture that must be recaptured) is the cleanest way to deflect.
After the defender is pulled away, immediately take the prize it was guarding.
Deflection often sets up a back-rank mate or wins a hanging piece.
Watch your own pieces too, so you are not the one being deflected.

B1000_1400.combos.attraction.intro: Attraction (also called a decoy) is the opposite of deflection: instead of chasing a piece away, you lure it onto a bad square. You often sacrifice material to drag the enemy king or a piece to a square where it can be checked, forked, or mated. The trick is that the capture looks free, but it walks the piece into your follow-up.

examples.title: Luring the king to the back rank
examples.explanation: Imagine a queen sacrifice that drags the black king to g8 or h8 with its escape squares blocked by the f7, g7 and h7 pawns. Once the king is fixed on the back rank, the rook delivers Rd8+ leading to mate. The idea: pull the king to where your remaining force can finish it.

keyPoints: Look for a sacrifice that the opponent is almost forced to accept, usually a check or a capture.
Aim to place the lured piece on a fork square, a mating square, or a pin.
Attracting the king into the open is a common way to start a mating attack.
Always calculate the follow-up before sacrificing; the decoy only works if the next move is strong.
Attraction and deflection are two sides of the same idea: control where enemy pieces stand.

B1000_1400.combos.capturing_defender.intro: Sometimes a piece you want to win is protected by exactly one defender. If you simply capture the target, you lose material to the recapture. The solution is to remove the guard first: capture or trade off the single defender, and then the target falls for free on the next move. This is also called 'removing the guard'.

examples.title: Identify guard and target
examples.explanation: This is a thinking exercise rather than a one-move win. Before grabbing material, scan the board for any enemy piece that is the sole defender of something important. The skill is to ask 'what guards this?' and remove that guard before taking the prize.

keyPoints: Identify the target you want and find its one key defender.
Take or trade the defender first, not the target.
After the defender is gone, the target is hanging; collect it.
This works for winning pieces and for clearing the guard of a mating square.
If the defender is more valuable than the target, removing it may not be worth it; count material first.

B1000_1400.combos.intermezzo.intro: An intermezzo, or zwischenzug ('in-between move'), is a surprise forcing move inserted before the move your opponent expects. Instead of automatically recapturing or completing an exchange, you first play a check or a bigger threat. Because that move must be answered, you change the situation and often come out ahead before returning to the original idea.

examples.title: Italian Game starting point
examples.explanation: From sharp openings like this, exchanges arise where the natural recapture is not best. Train yourself to pause and ask: is there a check or threat I can throw in first? That in-between move is the zwischenzug, and it often wins a tempo or a pawn.

keyPoints: Do not recapture on autopilot; first look for a more forcing move.
An in-between check or threat must be answered, so you gain time or material.
Common pattern: opponent captures, but before you recapture you give a check that wins something.
The most forcing in-between moves are checks, then threats to the queen, then big threats.
After the intermezzo is answered, you usually go back and finish the original exchange on better terms.

B1000_1400.mating.mate_in_two.intro: A mate in two is a forced checkmate that takes exactly two of your moves: you play move one, the opponent has any legal reply, and you mate on move two no matter what they do. The key skill is to find a first move that takes away the king's flight squares or sets up an unstoppable threat, then verify that every single defensive reply runs into mate.

examples.title: Forced mate in two: 1.Rd7!
examples.explanation: White plays 1.Rd7, seizing the seventh rank and the g7-mating idea. Now every reply loses: 1...h5 or 1...h6 allows 2.Qg7#, and 1...Kf8 allows 2.Qb8#. The rook controls the seventh rank while the queen mates, illustrating how two pieces cover all escape squares.

keyPoints: Look for the key first move, often a quiet move or a check that removes escape squares.
Check every legal black reply, not just the obvious one; all must lead to mate.
Use one piece to control the king's flight squares and another to deliver the blow.
A rook reaching the seventh or eighth rank often takes away the back-rank escapes.
If even one reply escapes, the move fails; keep searching for the true key.

B1000_1400.mating.sacrificial_attacks.intro: When the enemy king is poorly defended, you can sometimes give up material to rip open its shelter and force mate. A sacrificial mating attack trades points for time and exposure: by removing a key defensive pawn or piece, you open lines for your queen, rooks and bishops to swarm the king. The sacrifice is sound only if you can calculate a forced mate or an overwhelming attack afterward.

examples.title: Pressure against the castled king
examples.explanation: White's bishop on c4 eyes f7 and the knight on f3 can jump to attack. Positions like this are where sacrifices on f7 or h7 are tested: count attackers and defenders around the black king and ask whether opening lines leads to a forced attack.

keyPoints: Sacrifice to remove a defender or open a file or diagonal toward the king.
Bring more attackers than the opponent has defenders before committing.
After the sacrifice, play forcing moves (checks and threats) so the defense gets no time.
Calculate to a clear mate or decisive material gain; do not sacrifice on hope alone.
Classic targets are h7/h2 and f7/f2 near a castled king.

B1000_1400.endgame.kp_vs_k.intro: King and pawn versus a lone king is decided by the rule of key squares. For a non-rook pawn, the key squares sit two ranks ahead of the pawn (and on the sixth rank for an advanced pawn). If your king can reach a key square, the pawn promotes; if not, the defender can usually draw. Getting your king in front of the pawn, with the opposition, is the heart of the technique.

examples.title: King in front of the pawn: winning try
examples.explanation: White's king is ahead of the e-pawn. By advancing the king and using the opposition to push the black king back, White can reach a key square and escort the pawn to promotion. This is the winning setup to aim for.

examples.title: Pawn behind the king: defender holds
examples.explanation: Here the white king is not yet in front of its pawn and Black's king sits in the way. If the defender keeps taking the opposition and staying in front of the pawn, this type of position is drawn. It shows why the attacker must lead with the king, not the pawn.

keyPoints: Put your king in front of the pawn, not behind it, to clear a path.
If your king reaches a key square (generally two ranks ahead of the pawn), you win.
The opposition (kings facing with one square between, opponent to move) helps you make progress.
Defender's goal: keep the king directly in front of the pawn and take the opposition.
Rook pawns are a special case and are often only drawn because the king gets stalemated in the corner.

B1000_1400.endgame.outside_passed_pawn.intro: An outside passed pawn is a passer far away from the other pawns, usually on the opposite wing from where most material sits. Its power is as a decoy: the enemy king must rush over to stop it from queening, and while it is busy, your king marches in to gobble the pawns it left behind. You often sacrifice the outside passer to win on the other side.

examples.title: Queenside majority to create a passer
examples.explanation: With pawns balanced on the kingside and an extra possibility on the queenside, the side that creates a distant passed pawn there can decoy the enemy king. The lesson: an outside passer's job is to draw the king off so your king wins the kingside.

keyPoints: An outside passed pawn pulls the enemy king away from the rest of the board.
While their king chases the passer, your king invades the other wing.
You may give up the passer; the real prize is the pawns the king abandons.
A protected or far-advanced outside passer is even stronger.
Create one by trading on the side where you have a pawn majority.

B1000_1400.strategy.weak_squares_outpost.intro: A weak square is a square in the opponent's camp that can no longer be defended by a pawn, often because the pawns that would guard it have advanced or been traded. If such a square is also protected by one of your own pawns, it becomes an outpost: a perfect home for a knight. A knight planted on a central outpost, immune to pawn attacks, can dominate the position.

examples.title: Knight installed on e5
examples.explanation: White's knight sits on e5, supported by the d4 pawn. Black cannot easily kick it with a pawn, so it is a true outpost. From e5 the knight pressures Black's position and is hard to dislodge, a model of using a protected central square.

examples.title: Spotting the hole on e5
examples.explanation: Black's pawns on c5, d5, e6 and f7 no longer control e5, so e5 is a potential outpost for White. The plan is to maneuver a knight (for example Nf3-e5) into that hole where no black pawn can challenge it.

keyPoints: A weak square cannot be defended by an enemy pawn anymore.
An outpost is a weak square that your own pawn protects.
Knights love outposts on the fifth or sixth rank near the enemy king.
Keep your outpost knight; avoid trading it for a passive piece.
Look for holes created when the opponent pushes pawns (for example, a backward c-pawn leaving d5 weak).

B1000_1400.strategy.open_files.intro: An open file is a column with no pawns on it; a half-open file has pawns of only one color. Rooks are at their best on these files because they can travel up and down freely and invade the opponent's position, especially the seventh rank. Seizing an open file, and doubling rooks on it, is one of the most reliable plans in the middlegame.

examples.title: Contesting the open c-file
examples.explanation: The c-file is fully open. White's rook already occupies it with Rc1; doubling rooks or being first to control the file lets White invade on c7 or c8. Whoever owns the open file owns the initiative here.

examples.title: Rook on the seventh rank
examples.explanation: White's rook has reached the seventh rank on d7, where it attacks the a7 and b7 pawns and pins the king to its shelter. A rook on the seventh is the reward for winning an open file and is often worth a pawn or more in activity.

keyPoints: Place rooks on open or half-open files to maximize their range.
Doubling rooks (one behind the other) multiplies pressure on a file.
An open file is a highway to the seventh rank, where rooks attack pawns and trap the king.
Half-open files point at enemy pawns you can attack.
If both sides contest a file, the side that controls it can force trades or invade.

B1000_1400.strategy.good_vs_bad_bishop.intro: A bishop is called 'bad' when its own pawns are fixed on the same color squares it travels on, blocking its scope. A 'good' bishop has its pawns on the opposite color, so it moves freely. The difference matters most in closed positions and endgames. If you have the bad bishop, try to free it, trade it off, or place your pawns on the other color; if you face a bad bishop, keep the pawns fixed.

examples.title: Good vs bad in a locked center
examples.explanation: The pawns on d4/e5 versus d5/e6 lock the center on the light squares. A bishop hemmed in behind pawns of its own color (a light-squared bishop stuck behind d5/e6 for Black) is the bad one, while a bishop operating outside the chain is good. Aim to keep your bishop active and the enemy's blocked.

examples.title: Black's bad dark-squared bishop
examples.explanation: Black's bishop on e7 is boxed in by its own pawns on d5, e6 and f7. With limited squares it is a classic bad bishop, while White's freer pieces can target the light-square weaknesses. The plan against a bad bishop is to keep those pawns fixed so it stays passive.

keyPoints: Bad bishop: blocked by your own pawns on its color. Good bishop: pawns on the opposite color.
In closed and endgame positions, a good bishop can dominate a bad one.
Fix the enemy's pawns on the color of their bishop to make it bad.
If your bishop is bad, look to trade it or reroute it outside the pawn chain.
Place your own pawns on the opposite color of your bishop to give it room.

B1400_1800.tactics.interference.intro: Interference (also called obstruction) means placing a piece on a line so that a defender can no longer see what it is guarding. Long-range pieces - rooks, bishops, and queens - defend along ranks, files, and diagonals; if you cut that line with an interposing piece, the defended target is suddenly loose. The move often looks crazy because you may be sacrificing the interposing piece, but it works because the defender cannot capture and keep its defensive job at the same time. Look for two enemy pieces communicating along one line and a square in between where you can land with a threat.

examples.title: Cut the file with the bishop
examples.explanation: Both rooks contest the d-file and the black king is exposed. The white bishop on d5 sits on the line between the rooks; ideas like landing a piece on d-file squares to sever the black rook's contact with d1 illustrate how interference breaks the defender's connection. The point to see is that a piece interposed on a defended line can split a defender from its duty.

keyPoints: Interference blocks the line between a defender and the thing it protects.
It usually targets long-range pieces (rooks, bishops, queens) defending along a line.
The interposing move is often a sacrifice - it works because the defender cannot both capture and keep guarding.
Hunt for two enemy pieces 'talking' along one line and an in-between square you can occupy with a threat.

B1400_1800.tactics.xray_battery.intro: A battery is two pieces of the same kind of line lined up behind each other on an open file, rank, or diagonal - for example a queen behind a rook, or two rooks doubled - so their force is combined. An x-ray (or 'skewer-like' attack through a piece) is when one of your pieces exerts influence through an enemy piece along the same line, so that if the blocking piece moves or is removed, you hit what is behind it. Both ideas exploit the fact that long-range pieces act along whole lines, not just one square. Set up batteries to overpower a point, and use x-rays to win material once the front piece is dealt with.

examples.title: Queen-rook battery on the d-file
examples.explanation: White has a queen on d1 and rook on e1; doubling on the d-file (rook to d-file in front of the queen, or queen behind the rook) creates a battery that overwhelms the black rook on the open file. The lesson is how stacking two pieces on one line multiplies pressure against a single defended point.

examples.title: X-ray through the enemy rook
examples.explanation: White's rook on d1 and queen on d2 both bear down the d-file; the white queen 'x-rays' the black rook on d8 through White's own rook. If the front pieces are exchanged, the line stays loaded against d8. Notice how value lined up along a file lets you win the exchange or pile up on the target.

keyPoints: A battery is two like-moving pieces stacked on one open line to combine their power.
Put the lower-value piece in front when you want to attack; the heavy piece supports from behind.
An x-ray attacks (or defends) a square through an enemy piece sitting on the same line.
Open and half-open lines are the natural home for both batteries and x-rays.
Once the front piece is removed or moves, the rear piece collects what was behind it.

B1400_1800.tactics.quiet_move.intro: A quiet move is a move that gives no check and makes no capture, yet creates a threat the opponent cannot meet. They are the hardest tactics to find because our eyes are trained to look first at forcing moves. Often a quiet move comes at the end of a combination: you have set up a position where a simple, unhurried move threatens an unstoppable mate or a winning capture next. The skill is to keep calculating after the obvious checks and captures run out, and to verify that the threat really cannot be parried.

examples.title: Improve the queen, then strike
examples.explanation: There is no immediate check or capture that wins, but a calm repositioning of the queen toward the black king (for instance setting up threats against g7/h7 with support) prepares a decisive follow-up. The point is to recognise that a non-forcing move can be the strongest move when it builds an unanswerable threat.

keyPoints: A quiet move neither checks nor captures, yet poses a threat that cannot be stopped.
Keep looking after the forcing moves run out - the winning move is often the calm one.
Quiet moves frequently finish combinations rather than start them.
Always confirm the created threat is genuinely unstoppable before committing.

B1400_1800.tactics.overloaded_pieces.intro: A piece is overloaded when it is the only defender of two important things at once - it simply cannot be in two places. You exploit this by attacking one of its duties: when it deals with that, the other duty is abandoned and you win material or deliver mate. The first step is to spot a single enemy piece carrying too many jobs, such as guarding a back-rank square and a hanging piece at the same time. Then find the move that forces it to choose.

examples.title: The rook cannot guard two lines
examples.explanation: Black's rook on e8 must both contest the e-file and watch the back rank. White's rooks on d1 and e1 can pressure both duties; whichever job the black rook abandons, White exploits. The idea is to identify the single overworked defender and attack the duty that frees the bigger gain.

keyPoints: An overloaded piece is the sole defender of two targets simultaneously.
Attack one of its duties to pull it off the other.
Common overloads: a piece guarding both the back rank and a loose piece.
After the defender is deflected, collect the now-undefended target.

B1400_1800.endgame.lucena.intro: The Lucena position is the most important winning method in rook endgames: with a rook and a pawn on the seventh rank versus a lone rook, the stronger side wins by 'building a bridge.' The problem is that the defending rook keeps checking your king from behind; the solution is to use your rook as a shield. You first place your rook on the fourth rank (counting from your side), then walk your king out to give the pawn room, and finally interpose your rook to block the last check so the pawn promotes. Learning this position turns many drawn-looking endings into wins.

examples.title: Classic Lucena setup
examples.explanation: White (Kb8, pawn b7, rook c1) wants to promote, but the black rook on a2 will check the king as it emerges. The winning plan is Rc4 (rook to the fourth rank), then bring the king out toward the checks, and finally use the rook on the fourth rank as a bridge to block the decisive check. This is the textbook position to memorise.

keyPoints: Lucena is a win: rook plus pawn on the seventh versus a lone rook, with your king in front of the pawn.
Place your rook on the fourth rank to prepare the bridge.
March your king out toward the checking rook to make luft for the pawn.
Interpose the rook (the 'bridge') to block the final check and promote.
If your king is cut off too far from the pawn, the Lucena method may not apply - this is the core winning technique.

B1400_1800.endgame.philidor.intro: The Philidor position is the key drawing method when you are a pawn down in a rook endgame and the enemy pawn has not yet reached the sixth rank. The defending side keeps the rook on its third rank (the rank in front of where the enemy pawn would advance to attack), preventing the opposing king from gaining a safe shelter ahead of the pawn. The moment the pawn finally advances to the sixth rank, the defending rook drops to the back and checks the enemy king from behind, where it can never escape the checks. Knowing Philidor lets you hold many endings that look lost.

examples.title: Third-rank defence
examples.explanation: The defender keeps the rook active on the third rank (from the defender's side) so the stronger king cannot advance in front of its pawn. As long as the pawn has not reached the sixth rank, the rook patrols that rank; once the pawn pushes, the rook swings behind to check endlessly. This illustrates the holding idea of the Philidor draw.

keyPoints: Philidor is a draw when defending a pawn down before the enemy pawn reaches the sixth rank.
Hold your rook on the third rank (from your side) to deny the enemy king a shelter.
When the pawn finally advances to the sixth, switch to checking from behind.
Checks from far behind the pawn are safe because the king cannot hide from them.
Keep your king in front of the pawn whenever possible - this is the defender's main resource.

B1400_1800.endgame.rook_on_seventh.intro: A rook on the seventh rank (the opponent's second rank) is one of the most powerful endgame assets. From there it attacks the enemy pawns still on their starting squares and pins the enemy king to the back rank, restricting it badly. The rook is 'pig on the seventh' that gobbles pawns while the opponent struggles to defend. Doubling two rooks on the seventh is often decisive, threatening perpetual checks or mate. Activate your rook to the seventh whenever you can, especially combined with a passed pawn.

examples.title: Harvesting pawns on the seventh
examples.explanation: White's rook on a7 attacks Black's pawns along the seventh rank and ties the king to defence. The active rook collects material while the black pieces stay passive. The takeaway is that a rook on the seventh both wins pawns and confines the enemy king.

examples.title: Doubling toward the seventh
examples.explanation: White's rooks on a6 and b7 coordinate to dominate the enemy's back ranks; bringing both rooks to the seventh rank ('doubled pigs') is often crushing, threatening the king and the remaining pawns at once. This shows the power of two rooks working together near the enemy king.

keyPoints: The seventh rank attacks the enemy's un-advanced pawns and cramps the king.
An active rook on the seventh is usually worth more than a passive defensive rook.
Two rooks doubled on the seventh ('pigs') can be decisive, threatening mate or perpetual.
Combine the seventh-rank rook with a passed pawn to overload the defence.
Prefer activity: a rook on the seventh often justifies giving up a pawn elsewhere.

B1400_1800.calc.forcing_moves_first.intro: Good calculation starts by examining the most forcing moves - checks, captures, and threats - before quieter options. Forcing moves limit your opponent's replies, which makes the tree of variations small enough to calculate accurately. Train yourself to ask, in order: what checks do I have, what captures, and what threats? Even when the forcing move turns out not to work, looking first keeps you from missing tactics and sharpens your visualisation several moves deep.

examples.title: Scan checks, captures, threats
examples.explanation: Before choosing a plan, list White's forcing options: the bishop on c4 eyes f7 near the king, and the rooks control central files. Practising the checks-captures-threats scan here trains the habit of calculating the sharpest moves first, even in a calm-looking position.

keyPoints: Always look at checks, captures, and threats before quiet moves.
Forcing moves narrow the opponent's replies, making calculation manageable.
Use a consistent order: checks, then captures, then threats.
Calculate the most forcing line to its end before switching to another.
Even failed forcing moves are worth checking - they reveal tactics and resources.

B1400_1800.calc.candidate_moves.intro: Strong players do not calculate one move endlessly; they first generate a short list of candidate moves - usually two or three serious options - and then compare them. Casting a wide enough net stops you from fixating on the first idea you see, while keeping the list short stops you from drowning in possibilities. Once you have candidates, calculate each just far enough to compare, eliminate the worst, and commit to the best. Trust the move you calculated rather than second-guessing at the last moment.

examples.title: Pick two or three candidates
examples.explanation: This Italian-style middlegame offers several reasonable plans (castling, central pawn breaks, piece improvement). The exercise is to name two or three concrete candidate moves, weigh them against each other, discard the weakest, and choose - rather than playing the first move that catches the eye.

keyPoints: Generate two or three candidate moves before calculating deeply.
A short, well-chosen list prevents both tunnel vision and overload.
Calculate each candidate only far enough to compare them fairly.
Eliminate the clearly worse moves, then commit to the best.
Trust your calculation - avoid last-second changes without a concrete reason.

B1400_1800.openings.white_repertoire.intro: A repertoire is a set of openings you know and replay, so you reach familiar middlegames instead of improvising from move one. As White you first choose a main first move - usually 1.e4 (open, tactical, fast development) or 1.d4 (closed, strategic, long-term pressure) - and build a consistent set of responses around it. The goal is not to memorise endless theory but to understand the key tabiyas (standard positions) and the typical middlegame plan that follows. Pick one first move, learn it well, and aim for structures you enjoy playing.

examples.title: 1.e4 main line: the Ruy Lopez
examples.explanation: After 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bb5, White builds pressure on e5 and develops naturally - a model 1.e4 repertoire choice. The point is to commit to a first move and learn its standard setup and plan rather than play randomly.

examples.title: 1.d4 main line: Queen's Gambit Declined structure
examples.explanation: After 1.d4 d5 2.c4 e6 3.Nc3 Nf6, White gets a solid central grip and clear plans (minority attack, central play). This shows the alternative 1.d4 path - choose the first move whose resulting positions suit your style.

keyPoints: Choose one consistent first move (1.e4 or 1.d4) and build around it.
1.e4 tends to be open and tactical; 1.d4 tends to be closed and strategic.
Learn the key tabiyas (standard positions) rather than memorising endless lines.
Always know the typical middlegame plan that your opening produces.
Pick structures you understand and enjoy - a played repertoire beats a memorised one.

B1400_1800.openings.black_vs_e4.intro: As Black you need a reliable, repeatable answer to 1.e4, the most common first move. Sound choices include 1...e5 (classical, fights for the centre symmetrically), the Caro-Kann (1...c6, solid and structurally sturdy), the French (1...e6, resilient with clear pawn breaks), and the Sicilian (1...c5, ambitious and counter-attacking). The aim is to reach a comfortable structure you understand and to sidestep the early traps that punish unprepared players. Choose one defence, learn its main ideas and pawn breaks, and stick with it.

examples.title: Caro-Kann: solid and sound
examples.explanation: After 1.e4 c6 2.d4 d5, Black challenges the centre directly while keeping a healthy pawn structure and easy development for the light-squared bishop. A good example of a low-risk, understandable answer to 1.e4.

examples.title: Sicilian: ambitious counterplay
examples.explanation: After 1.e4 c5, Black declines symmetry and fights for the initiative on the queenside, often leading to sharp, double-edged play. It illustrates the more combative end of the anti-1.e4 spectrum.

keyPoints: Pick one sound defence to 1.e4 and learn it thoroughly.
1...e5 is classical; the Caro-Kann and French are solid; the Sicilian is ambitious.
Aim for a pawn structure you understand and feel comfortable in.
Know the typical pawn breaks (for example ...c5/...e5 freeing moves) for your choice.
Learn the early traps in your line so you are not caught off guard.

B1400_1800.openings.black_vs_d4.intro: To complete the repertoire, Black also needs a system against 1.d4. Popular, reliable choices include the Queen's Gambit Declined (1...d5 with ...e6, very solid), the Slav (1...d5 with ...c6, sturdy and active), and the King's Indian Defence (1...Nf6 and ...g6, hyper-modern with kingside counterattack). Ideally your anti-1.d4 choice meshes with your anti-1.e4 setup so you face familiar structures. Focus on understanding the central pawn breaks - typically ...c5 or ...e5 for Black - that free your position.

examples.title: Slav Defence: solid and flexible
examples.explanation: After 1.d4 d5 2.c4 c6, Black supports the d5-pawn without locking in the light-squared bishop, keeping a healthy structure and clear development. A dependable, low-maintenance answer to 1.d4.

examples.title: King's Indian Defence: counterattacking setup
examples.explanation: After 1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 g6 3.Nc3 Bg7 4.e4 d6, Black concedes the centre temporarily, then strikes back with ...e5 or ...c5 and a kingside pawn storm. It shows the dynamic, counterattacking style available against 1.d4.

keyPoints: Pick a sound defence to 1.d4 (QGD, Slav, or King's Indian are all reliable).
Try to coordinate it with your anti-1.e4 setup for familiar structures.
Understand the freeing pawn breaks - usually ...c5 or ...e5 for Black.
Solid systems (QGD/Slav) trade risk for sturdiness; the King's Indian trades safety for counterattack.
Know your line's middlegame plan, not just the opening moves.

B1800_2000.strategy.prophylactic_thinkin.intro: Prophylaxis means preventing the opponent's plan before you push your own. Strong players regularly stop and ask, 'If it were my opponent's move, what would they most want to do?' Then they look for a move that quietly takes that idea away while still improving their position. Good prophylaxis is invisible: the opponent never gets the break, the maneuver, or the active piece they were counting on.

examples.title: Quiet maneuvering, no tactics
examples.explanation: A balanced position with no forcing moves. Instead of grabbing space at random, ask what Black wants: activity for the c8-bishop and the e6/e5 break. White can prepare e3-e4 and reroute a rook to c1, restraining Black's counterplay before expanding. The point is to think about the opponent's intentions first.

examples.title: Restrain before you advance
examples.explanation: Black would like ...b4 hitting the knight and ...d5 breaking the center. White's calm moves (a2-a3 to meet ...b4, or f2-f3 to support e4 and blunt the b7-bishop) take the sting out of Black's plans first. This is prophylaxis: deny the break, then improve.

keyPoints: Before every move, ask what the opponent wants to do next and why.
A prophylactic move removes the opponent's idea while keeping or improving your own position.
Common targets to prevent: a freeing pawn break, an outpost, a piece that wants a great square, or a rook lift.
Do not become passive; restrain the opponent's best plan, then return to your own.
Often the best prophylaxis is a small move (a luft pawn, a defensive regroup) that costs almost nothing.

B1800_2000.strategy.zugzwang_triangulati.intro: Zugzwang is a position where any move a player makes worsens their position; they would prefer to pass, but the rules force a move. Triangulation is the technique of using your king (or another piece) to lose a tempo on purpose, returning to the same position but with the opponent to move, so they fall into zugzwang. These ideas decide many king-and-pawn and other simple endgames where the side forced to move must give way.

examples.title: Pure opposition / mutual zugzwang
examples.explanation: Kings face off with one square between them. The side NOT to move holds the opposition; the side to move must step aside and let the enemy king advance. This is the simplest model of zugzwang: being forced to move is a liability.

examples.title: Triangulation to pass the move
examples.explanation: White wants Black to be on move in this structure. By walking the king on a short triangle (for example Ke3-Kd3-back) White can reach the same position with Black to move, forcing Black's king to give ground so the pawn can advance. The detour 'loses' exactly one tempo.

examples.title: The defender squeezed
examples.explanation: With the kings already in opposition and a pawn behind, the side to move is in trouble: any king step lets the stronger side outflank and escort the pawn. Feeling who is hurt by having to move is the heart of zugzwang.

keyPoints: Zugzwang = the obligation to move is itself a disadvantage.
It appears mostly in endgames, where one quiet move can ruin a defense.
Triangulation means taking a three-square king path to return to the same position having 'lost' a move.
To triangulate you need a spare square your king can detour to that the opponent's king cannot match.
Recognize reciprocal (mutual) zugzwang: whoever is to move loses or must concede.

B1800_2000.endgame.r2_vs_r.intro: Rook and two pawns against a rook is usually winning, but only with accurate technique. The standard winning method is to advance the pawns together with the king's support while keeping your rook active, and to be alert for the defender's fortress and stalemate tricks. The defender's best chance is to give checks, blockade the pawns, or sacrifice the rook for both pawns to reach a drawn single-rook or king-and-pawn ending.

examples.title: Connected passers, active rook
examples.explanation: White's two connected pawns plus an active rook give a winning advantage. The plan: bring the king up, advance b- and c-pawns together so they defend each other, and keep the rook from passivity. Black's lone rook cannot stop both pawns and check forever.

examples.title: Rook supports from the side
examples.explanation: With the rook active and the king ready to advance, White marches the connected pawns up the board. Note the technique of not rushing: improve the king first, neutralize checks, then push. The defender will try to trade rooks or win a pawn to reach a draw.

keyPoints: Connected passed pawns are far easier to convert than split pawns; advance them side by side.
Keep your rook active behind the pawns or cutting the enemy king off; a passive rook draws.
Use your king to shepherd the pawns once they are safe from rook checks.
Watch for the defender's fortress (rook blockading in front) and for stalemate when pushing the last pawn.
Do not blunder a pawn for a check; one pawn down this endgame is often only a draw.

B1800_2000.endgame.queen_endgames.intro: Queen endgames are dominated by checks: the queen is so powerful that the defender can often give endless checks (perpetual check) to draw even when down material. To win you must shelter your king from checks, use the queen to escort a passed pawn, and be willing to trade queens into a won king-and-pawn ending when safe. Judging whether a position is a perpetual-check draw or a real win is the central skill.

examples.title: Queen vs lone king technique
examples.explanation: Queen and king versus king is a forced mate (here mate in seven with best play). The method: use the queen a knight's-move away to herd the king to the edge, bring your king up, and avoid stalemate. This builds the queen-handling instincts every queen endgame needs.

examples.title: Escorting a passed pawn
examples.explanation: White's far-advanced c-pawn is the trump. With the queen supporting promotion and shielding the king from checks, White queens the pawn and wins (a verified forced win). The defender's only hope is perpetual check, which the queen and pawn together can extinguish.

examples.title: Mating net / perpetual awareness
examples.explanation: An exposed king is fatal in queen endings: here Black mates in one with Qg2#. The lesson cuts both ways. When you are the stronger side, drive the king into the open; when defending, keep your king sheltered and look for perpetual check.

keyPoints: The queen's checking power means many material-down positions are saved by perpetual check.
To win, find a safe haven for your king where checks run out, often using your own pawns as a screen.
A passed pawn plus queen wins by advancing the pawn while the king hides from checks behind it.
Trading queens is often the cleanest path to victory if the resulting pawn ending is winning.
Always check whether the defender has a perpetual before celebrating an extra pawn.

B1800_2000.endgame.conversion_technique.intro: Having a winning advantage is not the same as winning; you still have to convert it. Good technique means trading pieces (not pawns) to reduce the opponent's chances, shutting down any counterplay before grabbing more material, and steering toward a simple, clearly won endgame. The motto is 'do not hurry': improve your worst piece, keep your king safe, and only then cash in. Most won games are thrown away by impatience and allowing counterplay.

examples.title: Up a clean piece, no tactics
examples.explanation: White is a full knight ahead with no immediate tactic. Correct technique: centralize the king and knight, then advance the queenside pawn majority to make a passed pawn while the extra piece dominates. No risk-taking is needed; just improve and convert.

examples.title: Up the exchange
examples.explanation: White has rook against bishop with kings safe (luft on both sides). The plan is textbook conversion: bring the king up the board, restrict the bishop, and use the rook's extra power to win pawns or create a passer. Avoid loosening moves that give the bishop activity.

keyPoints: When ahead in material, trade pieces to simplify; avoid trading your last pawns when you need them to win.
Prevent counterplay first: secure your king and blockade the opponent's active pieces before pushing for more.
Aim for a position your technique can win cleanly rather than the flashiest continuation.
Improve your worst-placed piece and your king before committing.
Stay patient: 'do not hurry' avoids the blunders that rescue lost positions.

B1800_2000.tactics.mate_in_four_five.intro: Deeper forced mates (four or five moves) test your ability to calculate a long sequence without losing the thread. The key is to keep the king in a net with forcing moves (checks, captures, and threats), and to account for every defensive try at each step. Counting the king's escape squares and confirming that none open up is what separates a real forced mate from a hopeful attack. Use the most forcing move order so the defender has the fewest replies.

examples.title: Philidor's Legacy (smothered mate in 4)
examples.explanation: A verified forced mate in four: 1.Nf7+ Kg8 2.Nh6+ Kh8 3.Qg8+ Rxg8 4.Nf7#. The queen sacrifice forces the rook to block its own king, and the knight delivers smothered mate. Every black reply is forced, which is what makes the long line calculable.

examples.title: Forcing rook sacrifice to mate
examples.explanation: A shorter forced finish that shows the technique: 1.Rxh7+ Kxh7 2.Qh5# (a verified mate). The capture rips open the h-file and the queen mates with no escape. Spotting that the sacrifice is forced and the follow-up is mate is the core calculation skill.

keyPoints: Calculate with forcing moves first: checks and captures narrow the opponent's choices.
At each move, list every legal defense and make sure your mate still works against all of them.
Track the defending king's escape squares; mate requires zero escapes.
Choose the move order that gives the opponent the fewest options.
Visualize the final mating picture before you start, then steer the king toward it.

B1800_2000.tactics.very_long_lines.intro: Some combinations are not mating attacks but long forcing sequences that end in a clear material or positional gain. The challenge is to calculate the whole line to its conclusion and correctly evaluate the final position, since one overlooked in-between move or recapture can reverse the verdict. Anchor your calculation on forcing moves, hold the resulting picture in your head, and look hard for the opponent's best resource at the very end before committing.

examples.title: Forcing exchanges to a clear edge
examples.explanation: A tangle of hanging pieces. White must calculate the captures and checks to the end (for example resolving the c3-queen and e4-knight) and confirm the resulting position is better. The point is to follow the forcing moves until things settle, then judge the outcome.

examples.title: A forced combination to mate
examples.explanation: White has a verified forced win: 1.Nf6+ gxf6 2.Bxf7#. Even short lines train the habit of pushing the calculation to its true end. Confirm the king cannot escape and that intermediate captures do not give the defender a saving resource.

keyPoints: Calculate the forcing line all the way to a quiet position before deciding it is good.
Re-evaluate the final position carefully; the gain at the end is what matters, not the fireworks.
Watch for the opponent's in-between moves (zwischenzug) that change the result.
Keep a running count of material and threats so the endpoint evaluation is accurate.
Identify the single resource (a defensive trick or a saving check) that the whole line hinges on.

B1800_2000.attack.opposite_castling.intro: When the kings castle on opposite wings, both sides can throw their pawns forward to open lines against the enemy king without exposing their own. This creates a race: whoever breaks through first usually wins. Speed and open files matter more than material, so you push the pawns in front of the enemy king, avoid moving the pawns that shelter your own king, and bring rooks and the queen to the files you are prying open. Judging who is faster is the decisive skill.

examples.title: Mirror setup, race ahead
examples.explanation: Both kings sit on the queenside with symmetrical structures, so each side will push pawns at the other king. White prepares a kingside-style storm against c8 (a- and b-pawns) while watching Black's counter-push. The plan is to open a file toward the enemy king faster than the opponent.

examples.title: Pawn storm underway
examples.explanation: White has begun advancing on the side where Black's king lives; the next steps are to lever open a file with a pawn break and pile rooks and queen onto it. Note that White keeps the queenside pawns near its own king intact. Whoever opens lines first will be the one attacking.

keyPoints: Storm the pawns that face the enemy king; do not move your own king's shelter pawns.
It is a race: opening files and diagonals quickly is worth more than a pawn.
Use pawn levers (like h- and g-pawns) to force open a file, then load rooks and queen onto it.
Count tempi for both attacks to judge who breaks through first.
If your attack is slower, switch to defense or seek to blunt the opponent's break.

B1800_2000.attack.sacrifice_for_initiati.intro: Not every sacrifice leads to a forced win; many are made for the initiative, giving up material for lasting pressure, active pieces, and an opponent stuck on defense. Judging these positional sacrifices is about intuition and general factors rather than a single calculated line: lead in development, exposed enemy king, weak squares, and the inability of the defender to untangle. The compensation is real when the opponent can never get comfortable and you keep setting fresh problems.

examples.title: Pressure-based play in a sound structure
examples.explanation: A typical setup where one side may later sacrifice a pawn (for example with a central or minority break) to open lines and seize the initiative. The question is always whether activity and pressure outweigh the material, not whether there is a forced win.

examples.title: A gambit pawn for initiative
examples.explanation: Black has offered a wing pawn (a Benko-style idea) to open lines on the queenside and gain long-term activity for the rooks and bishop. The compensation is positional pressure, not an immediate recovery of material. Evaluating such pawn sacrifices is exactly this lesson's theme.

keyPoints: A sacrifice for the initiative buys time, activity, and pressure rather than a forced mate or regain.
Compensation factors: development lead, exposed king, open lines, weak squares, and passive enemy pieces.
Keep making threats so the opponent stays on defense and cannot consolidate the extra material.
You usually cannot calculate it to the end; evaluate the resulting position with general judgment.
If the opponent untangles and your activity fades, the sacrifice has failed, so press while the iron is hot.
